parent.label: Code & Math
parent.description: Structured, formal, verifiable knowledge. Code has unambiguous semantics — it either runs or it doesn't. Mathematics has proofs. This is where models learn to reason precisely, to work through constraint satisfaction, and to follow chains of implication without drifting. The overrepresentation of code relative to its internet share is intentional: it punches above its weight in teaching formal thinking.
bullets: GitHub alone hosts 150M+ public repositories across hundreds of programming languages
Mathematical LaTeX source preserves symbolic structure that PDF rendering loses
Code is among the most deduplicated data — boilerplate and generated code is aggressively filtered

cells.label: Code (GitHub)
cells.description: Public repositories on GitHub, GitLab, and similar platforms — the world's largest archive of executable human reasoning. Source code in Python, JavaScript, C, Rust, SQL, Bash, and hundreds of other languages, plus the surrounding metadata: commit messages, README files, docstrings, and issue threads.
bullets: The Stack (BigCode): 3TB+ of permissively-licensed code filtered for quality and deduplication
Linux kernel: 30+ years, 30M+ lines, contributions from 25,000+ developers — one of the most-analyzed single artifacts
Commit messages and docstrings teach the relationship between code and its intent in natural language
cells.screenshotCaption: Linux kernel on GitHub — 30+ years of collaborative programming

cells.label: Stack Overflow
cells.description: Structured Q&A where programmers ask specific questions and receive voted, curated answers. The format is almost a mini-proof: problem statement, failed attempt, correct solution, explanation of why it works. Error messages paired with fixes are among the most transferable training signals.
bullets: 58M+ questions spanning 170+ programming tags, going back to 2008
Voting surfaces the best explanations, creating a quality signal unavailable in raw crawl data
Error message → solution pairs teach debugging and error interpretation
cells.screenshotCaption: Stack Overflow — the internet's most-voted programmer Q&A archive

cells.label: arXiv Math/CS
cells.description: Formal mathematical proofs and theoretical computer science, often in LaTeX source. This gives models the symbolic notation of mathematics — ∀, ∃, ⊢, → — along with proof techniques: induction, contradiction, construction, and the careful use of 'assume' and 'it follows that.'
bullets: arXiv hosts 2M+ papers in math, CS, physics, and quantitative biology
LaTeX source (not just PDFs) preserves equation structure and symbolic relationships
Proof techniques — induction, contradiction, construction — appear as recurring textual patterns
cells.screenshotCaption: arXiv math paper — formal proofs with LaTeX source

cells.label: MathOverflow
cells.description: Expert-level mathematical discussion where PhD researchers ask and answer research-level questions. Among the densest concentrations of formal mathematical reasoning on the open internet — linking symbolic notation to natural-language explanation of why it works.
bullets: 95,000+ questions, predominantly research-level pure and applied mathematics
Experts explaining why proofs fail, the intuition behind theorems, edge cases
Unique for connecting formal notation with conversational mathematical explanation
